topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a saccharine wave washing over the crowded reception hall. I leaned against a marble pillar, trying to make myself small, invisible. Then, I saw him. Standing near the buffet, laughing with the groom’s cousin, was Liam. My stomach tightened, a familiar, unpleasant clenching. He looked… well, he looked exactly the same, only polished, happier, and utterly undeserving of my current state of barely contained irritation. I forced a slow sip of my lukewarm champagne, focusing intently on the condensation dripping down the glass. I mentally cataloged the various escape routes, the quickest paths to a quiet corner where I could simply cease existing for a while. When our eyes briefly met across the room, a sharp, involuntary flinch ran down my spine. He offered a small, practiced smile, and I managed a tight, dismissive nod before pivoting sharply toward the exit.

[story 2]

“Oh, look who it is,” Sarah chirped, nudging me with her elbow. I didn't need to turn around to know who she meant. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. Liam. He was across the room, looking annoyingly relaxed in a crisp suit, holding court near the dance floor. Every muscle in my neck felt suddenly rigid. I took a deep, deliberate breath, inhaling the overly sweet scent of lilies and expensive cologne. I pretended to be deeply engrossed in examining the floral arrangements, my gaze fixed on a particularly gaudy centerpiece. When he caught my eye—and I felt the heat rise instantly to my cheeks—I deliberately lowered my gaze, my expression carefully blank, bordering on frosty. I focused on the pattern of the carpet, willing myself to remain utterly unaffected by his presence. It was exhausting having to maintain this level of stillness.

[story 3]

I was nursing a lukewarm drink near the edge of the patio, strategically positioned for maximum peripheral vision and minimum engagement. The noise of the celebration was overwhelming, a constant barrage of forced cheerfulness. Then, there he was. Liam. He caught my attention, and a distinct sense of internal friction sparked within me. I felt a distinct urge to physically retreat, to put distance between us as if a pane of glass had just been installed. I watched him move, observing the easy camaraderie he shared with everyone, and a sour taste coated the back of my throat. When he happened to glance in my direction, I met his gaze for a fraction of a second too long. My expression remained stubbornly neutral, a carefully constructed mask of polite indifference, though inside, I was cataloging every single irritating detail of the scene, wishing for a sudden, merciful silence.
